content:
## Root Cause

`rustls-platform-verifier` on Android requires a real JVM + Android `Context` object obtained via JNI (see `android.rs` line 90 — the `global()` call panics when `GLOBAL` was never set). Termux runs as an ordinary Linux-like process with **no Java VM**, so the initialization call in `main.rs` is a no-op and the verifier is never actually set up. Every subsequent TLS handshake then hits the panic.

The fix has three parts: remove the unreachable `initialize()` call, stop pulling `rustls-platform-verifier` into the Android binary entirely by switching to `native-tls` for that target, and update the CI build accordingly.

---

### 1 — `Cargo.toml`

```toml
[package]
name = "gce-vm"
version = "1.0.12"
edition = "2021"
authors = ["Rodolfo Villaruz <rodolfo@yes.ph>"]
description = "A small command-line tool for managing a single Google Compute Engine VM you use as a development or work machine."
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/rodolfovillaruz/gvm"
readme = "README.md"
keywords = ["cli", "tool"]
categories = ["command-line-utilities"]
exclude = [".dump/*"]

[[bin]]
name = "gvm"
path = "src/main.rs"

# ── Shared dependencies (all platforms) ────────────────────────────────────────
[dependencies]
tokio      = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
clap       = { version = "4", features = ["derive", "env"] }
anyhow     = "1"
thiserror  = "2"
serde      = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
gcp_auth   = "0.12"
colored    = "3"
google-cloud-auth        = "1.9.0"
google-cloud-compute-v1  = "2.4.0"
google-cloud-gax         = "1.9.1"

# ── TLS — non-Android platforms: reqwest with rustls ───────────────────────────
# rustls-platform-verifier works correctly on Linux, macOS, and Windows because
# those targets never call into a JVM.
[target.'cfg(not(target_os = "android"))'.dependencies]
reqwest                  = { version = "0.13", features = ["json", "rustls"] }

# ── TLS — Android (Termux CLI binary) ─────────────────────────────────────────
# rustls-platform-verifier requires a live Java VM and an Android Context object
# obtained through JNI. A Termux process has neither; initialising the verifier
# is impossible and every TLS handshake panics. Use native-tls (vendored OpenSSL
# linked at compile time by cargo-ndk) instead.
[target.'cfg(target_os = "android")'.dependencies]
reqwest = { version = "0.13", default-features = false, features = ["json", "native-tls-vendored"] }
```

Key changes:
- **Removed** the single `reqwest` line and the direct `rustls-platform-verifier` dependency.
- **Added** two target-gated `reqwest` blocks: `rustls` everywhere else, `native-tls-vendored` on Android.
- `native-tls-vendored` compiles and statically links OpenSSL via the NDK, so the binary is fully self-contained and needs no Termux `openssl` package.

---

### 2 — `src/main.rs`

Remove the entire `rustls-platform-verifier` initialization block — it is unreachable on the only target that actually needs TLS initialization (Android) and is a no-op everywhere else:

```rust
use google_cloud_compute_v1::client::Instances;
use google_cloud_gax::paginator::ItemPaginator;
use std::env::var_os;
use std::process::Command;
use std::time::{Duration, Instant};

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    // ── removed: rustls_platform_verifier::initialize() ───────────────────────
    // rustls-platform-verifier requires JNI + an Android Context, which a Termux
    // CLI process never has. The crate is no longer used on Android at all; see
    // the target-specific reqwest dependency in Cargo.toml.

    let args: Vec<String> = std::env::args().collect();
    // ... rest of main unchanged ...
```

Everything from `find_instance`, `wait_for_ssh`, `credentials_available`, and the subcommand handlers stays exactly as written.

---

### 3 — `.github/workflows/release.yaml` — Android job

The `native-tls-vendored` feature compiles OpenSSL from source via the NDK, so the build step needs a few additions:

```yaml
      # ── NDK + cargo-ndk for Android ──────────────────────────────────────────
      - name: Setup Android NDK
        if: matrix.method == 'ndk'
        uses: nttld/setup-ndk@v1
        id: setup-ndk          # ← give it an id so we can read ndk.home below
        with:
          ndk-version: r26d

      - name: Install cargo-ndk
        if: matrix.method == 'ndk'
        run: cargo install cargo-ndk --locked

      # OpenSSL's build script (vendored via native-tls-vendored) needs Perl and
      # the NDK host toolchain on PATH.
      - name: Install Perl (required by vendored OpenSSL)
        if: matrix.method == 'ndk'
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y perl

      # ── Compile ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      - name: Build (cargo-ndk)
        if: matrix.method == 'ndk'
        env:
          ANDROID_NDK_HOME: ${{ steps.setup-ndk.outputs.ndk-path }}
        run: cargo ndk -t arm64-v8a build --release --locked
```

The `ANDROID_NDK_HOME` variable is what `openssl-sys` and `cc` use to locate the NDK toolchain during the vendored OpenSSL compilation.

---

### Why the other approaches don't work

| Approach | Why it fails in Termux |
|---|---|
| `rustls` + `rustls-platform-verifier::initialize()` | `initialize()` has no way to supply a `JavaVM` or `Context`; `GLOBAL` remains unset; panics on first handshake |
| `rustls` + `rustls-native-certs` | Not a public feature in reqwest 0.13; `rustls` feature unconditionally enables `rustls-platform-verifier` |
| `native-tls` (not vendored) | Requires Termux's `openssl` package at runtime; the binary is not self-contained |
| `native-tls-vendored` ✓ | OpenSSL compiled and statically linked at build time via the NDK; no JVM, no runtime dependency |
